data.title: New Data Shows A Surprising Rebound In Tech Hiring. Software Engineer Job Postings Are 'Rapidly Rising' And Are Up 11% Year Over Year
data.description:
For much of the past two years, the conversation around artificial intelligence has centered on one fear: that machines would soon start replacing white-collar workers at scale. But new labor market data suggests something very different may be happening.

Instead of disappearing, many tech jobs appear to be coming back. According to a new analysis from Citadel Securities, job postings for software engineers are “rapidly rising” and are now up about 11% year over year.

Tech Hiring Rebounds Despite AI Anxiety

The data, based on job postings tracked by job site Indeed, shows a clear rebound in demand for software engineers even as companies pour massive investments into artificial intelligence.

Don't Miss:

The broader job market has remained relatively steady as well. Citadel added that the unemployment rate recently printed at 4.28%, a level that still signals a resilient labor market.

At the same time, companies are spending aggressively on AI infrastructure. Citadel estimates that AI capital expenditures have reached about 2% of U.S. GDP, or roughly $650 billion. Commodities tied to the AI boom have surged too, with “AI-adjacent commodities” up about 65% since early 2023.

Despite the narrative that AI will eliminate large numbers of jobs, the firm argues the data doesn't support that view.

“In spite the current displacement narrative – job postings for software engineers are rising rapidly,” the report said.

The hiring trend comes as the U.S. prepares for a massive buildout of computing infrastructure. Almost 3,000 data centers are currently planned across the country, according to data analytics company Aterio, which is also boosting employment in construction and related industries.

AI Adoption Still Moving Slowly

Another reason mass job displacement hasn't appeared yet is that AI adoption is still developing gradually inside workplaces.

Data from the St. Louis Fed's Real-Time Population Survey released in November shows more people were experimenting with generative AI tools, but daily use for work remained relatively stable at just under 40%.

Citadel argues that the key question isn't simply whether people have tried AI, but how often they rely on it to perform their jobs.

“The more important question insofar as it relates to the AI displacement narrative is: how intensely is AI being used for work?” the report said.

So far, the numbers show little evidence of a sudden shift. The share of workers using generative AI every day for their jobs remains relatively low, suggesting companies are still figuring out how to integrate the technology.

Productivity Shock, Not Economic Collapse

Rather than triggering mass unemployment, the firm argues AI may end up functioning more like earlier technological revolutions.

Historically, technologies such as electricity, computers and the internal combustion engine boosted productivity, lowered costs and expanded economic output.

In many cases, they also created entirely new industries and business models.

That possibility is already visible in another piece of data highlighted in the report: a surge in new business formation.

Applications to start new businesses in the U.S. have risen sharply over the past decade, reaching levels far above those seen in the early 2000s. In January, there were 532,319 new business applications, an increase of 7.2% compared to December, per the U.S. Census Bureau.

According to Citadel, that trend could reflect the emergence of new economic opportunities created by digital tools and automation.

The firm also points out that fully replacing large numbers of workers with AI would require enormous computing resources, energy and infrastructure. If the cost of running those systems rises above the cost of human labor for certain tasks, companies have little incentive to automate them.

“It seems more likely that AI will be a complement rather than a substitute for labor in many areas,” the report said.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: What's Going On With Acurx Pharmaceuticals Stock Today?
data.description:
Acurx Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (NASDAQ:ACXP) shares are down during early trading on Friday following the company’s recent financial update, which revealed a net loss for the fourth quarter.

The company reported a net loss of 73 cents per diluted share, for the fourth quarter of 2025, beating the Street view of $1.65 loss.

Acurx ended the quarter with cash totaling $7.6 million, an increase from $3.7 million a year earlier, and raised approximately $1.5 million through its Equity Line of Credit during the fourth quarter.

The company is focused on developing a new class of antibiotics, with its lead candidate, Ibezapolstat, preparing for international Phase 3 clinical trials to treat C. difficile infection.

About The Company

Acurx Pharmaceuticals Inc is a clinical stage biopharmaceutical company developing a new class of antibiotics for infections caused by bacteria listed as priority pathogens by the World Health Organization, Centers for Disease Control and Prevention and Food and Drug Administration.

The company’s approach is to develop antibiotic candidates with a Gram-positive selective spectrum, focusing on addressing difficult-to-treat bacterial infections.

This focus is particularly relevant as antibiotic resistance continues to rise globally, making the development of new therapies crucial.

Earnings & Analyst Outlook

Acurx Pharmaceuticals, Inc. Common Stock is slated to provide its next financial update on May 12, 2026 (estimated).

  • EPS Estimate: -$1.24 (Up from -$2.20)

ACXP Price Action: Acurx Pharmaceuticals shares were down 19.16% at $4.24 at the time of publication on Friday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Photo: jittawit21/Shutterstock

data.title: Bitcoin's Cycle Is Intact, But The Bottom May Come In 2027, Analyst Says
data.description:
Bitcoin's (CRYPTO: BTC) four-year market cycle still appears intact, but 2026 may be too early to declare the end of the bear market, according to a prominent analyst.

Bitcoin Cycle Still Aligns With History

Benjamin Cowen said on Thursday that Bitcoin’s long-standing cycle pattern remains largely consistent with previous market cycles, despite claims on social media that the structure has broken.

According to Cowen, many investors believe the cycle failed because Bitcoin did not reach the extreme price targets they expected or because altcoins underperformed.

However, historical data suggests the cycle has followed a similar timeline to past market peaks.

Cowen noted that Bitcoin's major cycle tops have historically occurred in Q4 of the post-halving year, including 2013, 2017, 2021 and 2025.

Measured from cycle low to peak, the latest cycle topped around day 1,162, nearly identical to previous cycles, differing by roughly a week. This close alignment suggests the four-year cycle structure remains intact, he said.

While the current drawdown has been milder than some previous bear markets, Bitcoin's price behaviour still broadly mirrors historical patterns.

In past U.S. midterm election years, 2014, 2018 and 2022, Bitcoin typically bottomed in February, rallied into March and then moved lower again, a pattern Cowen believes could be repeating in 2026.

Indicators Suggest The Bottom May Not Be In

Cowen said some metrics hint that Bitcoin could be approaching a bottom, but several historically reliable indicators have not yet reached typical bear-market levels.

In previous cycles, major bottoms occurred when metrics such as the MVRV Z-score fell below zero, supply in profit and loss crossed, and price moved close to the balance price.

Those conditions have not fully materialized in the current cycle, he said.

Cowen added that bear markets often feature long upward trends followed by sudden breakdowns, making them difficult to identify in real time.

Given the historical timing of midterm-year cycles, he believes it is still too early in 2026 to confidently call a market bottom, even as bullish narratives gain traction.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: El Pollo Loco Posts Upbeat Q4 Results, Joins Kyivstar Group, American Public Education And Other Big Stocks Moving Higher On Friday
data.description:
U.S. stocks were higher, with the Dow Jones index gaining around 300 points on Friday.

Shares of El Pollo LoCo Holdings Inc (NASDAQ:LOCO) rose sharply during Friday's session after the company reported better-than-expected fourth-quarter financial results.

El Pollo Loco reported quarterly earnings of 25 cents per share which beat the analyst consensus estimate of 20 cents per share. The company reported quarterly sales of $123.515 million which beat the analyst consensus estimate of $122.783 million.

Also, Benchmark upgraded the stock from Hold to Buy.

El Pollo LoCo shares jumped 17.6% to $12.79 on Friday.

Here are some other big stocks recording gains in today’s session.

Photo via Shutterstock

data.title: $100 Invested In Petrobras Brasileiro 5 Years Ago Would Be Worth This Much Today
data.description:
Petrobras Brasileiro (NYSE:PBR) has outperformed the market over the past 5 years by 7.21% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 18.37%. Currently, Petrobras Brasileiro has a market capitalization of $121.28 billion.

Buying $100 In PBR: If an investor had bought $100 of PBR stock 5 years ago, it would be worth $230.10 today based on a price of $18.82 for PBR at the time of writing.

Petrobras Brasileiro's Performance Over Last 5 Years

Finally -- what's the point of all this? The key insight to take from this article is to note how much of a difference compounded returns can make in your cash growth over a period of time.

This article was generated by Benzinga's automated content engine and reviewed by an editor.
